From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Title and form of address for learned or skilled individual

"Fellowship" redirects here. For other uses, see [Fellowship (disambiguation)](/wiki/Fellowship_(disambiguation) "Fellowship (disambiguation)") and [Fellow (disambiguation)](/wiki/Fellow_(disambiguation) "Fellow (disambiguation)").

A **fellow** is a title and form of address for distinguished, learned, or skilled individuals in academia, medicine, research, and industry. The exact meaning of the term differs in each field. In [learned](/wiki/Learned_society "Learned society") or [professional](/wiki/Professional_society "Professional society") societies, the term refers to a privileged member who is specially elected in recognition of their work and achievements.[[1]](#cite_note-Lexico-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Collins-2) Within [institutions of higher education](/wiki/Institutions_of_higher_education "Institutions of higher education"), a fellow is a member of a highly ranked group of teachers at a particular college or university or a member of the governing body in some universities. It can also be a specially selected postgraduate student who has been appointed to a post (called a **fellowship**) granting a [stipend](/wiki/Stipend "Stipend"), research facilities and other privileges for a fixed period (usually one year or more) in order to undertake some advanced study or research, often in return for teaching services.[[1]](#cite_note-Lexico-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Collins-2) In the context of medical education in North America, a fellow (also known as a fellow physician) is a doctor who is undergoing a supervised, sub-specialty medical training (fellowship) after having completed a specialty training program (residency).[[3]](#cite_note-Tennessee2025-3) Lastly, In large, [R&D-intensive](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development") institutions, the term denotes a small number of senior scientists and engineers.

## Education and academia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Education and academia")]

In education and academia there are several kinds of fellowships, awarded for different reasons.

### Teaching fellowships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Teaching fellowships")]

Main article: [Teaching fellow](/wiki/Teaching_fellow "Teaching fellow")

The title of (senior) teaching fellow is used to denote an academic teaching position at a university or similar institution and is roughly equivalent to the title of (senior) [lecturer](/wiki/Lecturer "Lecturer"). The title *(senior) fellow* can also be bestowed to an academic member of staff upon retirement who continues to be affiliated to a university in the United Kingdom.

The term teaching fellow or [teaching assistant](/wiki/Teaching_assistant "Teaching assistant") is used, in the United States and United Kingdom, in secondary school, high school and middle school setting for students or adults that assist a teacher with one or more classes.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Medical fellowships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Medical fellowships")]

Main article: [Fellowship (medicine)](/wiki/Fellowship_(medicine) "Fellowship (medicine)")

In US medical institutions, a fellow (also known as a fellow physician) refers to someone who has completed residency training (e.g. in family medicine, internal medicine, pediatrics, general surgery, etc.) and is currently in a 1- to 3-year subspecialty training program (e.g. cardiology, sleep medicine/somnology, pediatric nephrology, transplant surgery, etc.).[[3]](#cite_note-Tennessee2025-3)

### Research fellowships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Research fellowships")]

#### As an academic position

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: As an academic position")]

Main article: [Research fellow](/wiki/Research_fellow "Research fellow")

The title of research fellow may be used to denote an [academic position](/wiki/Academic_position "Academic position") at a university or a similar institution; it is roughly equivalent to the title of [lecturer](/wiki/Lecturer "Lecturer") in the Commonwealth teaching career pathway.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### As a financial grant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: As a financial grant")]

Research fellow may also refer to the recipient of academic financial grant or [scholarship](/wiki/Scholarship "Scholarship").
For example, in Germany, institutions such as the [Alexander von Humboldt Foundation](/wiki/Alexander_von_Humboldt_Foundation "Alexander von Humboldt Foundation") offer research fellowship for postdoctoral research and refer to the holder as research fellows, while the award holder may formally hold a specific [academic title](/wiki/Academic_ranks "Academic ranks") at their home institution (e.g., *[Privatdozent](/wiki/Privatdozent "Privatdozent")*).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

These are often shortened to the name of the programme or organization, e.g. [Dorothy Hodgkin](/wiki/Dorothy_Hodgkin "Dorothy Hodgkin") Fellow rather than Dorothy Hodgkin Research Fellow, except where this might cause confusion with another fellowship, (e.g. [Royal Society University Research Fellowship](/wiki/Royal_Society_University_Research_Fellowship "Royal Society University Research Fellowship").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-urf-8)[[9]](#cite_note-urfs-9))

In the context of [graduate school](/wiki/Graduate_school "Graduate school") in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), a fellow is a recipient of a [postgraduate](/wiki/Postgraduate "Postgraduate") fellowship. Examples include the [NSF Graduate Research Fellowship](/wiki/NSF-GRF "NSF-GRF"), the [DoD National Defense Science and Engineering Graduate Fellowship](/wiki/DoD_NDSEG_Fellowship "DoD NDSEG Fellowship"), the [DOE Computational Science Graduate Fellowship](/wiki/Computational_Science_Graduate_Fellowship "Computational Science Graduate Fellowship"), the [Guggenheim Fellowship](/wiki/Guggenheim_Fellowship "Guggenheim Fellowship"), the [Rosenthal Fellowship](/wiki/Harold_Wallace_Rosenthal "Harold Wallace Rosenthal"), the [Frank Knox Memorial Fellowship](/wiki/Frank_Knox_Memorial_Fellowship "Frank Knox Memorial Fellowship"), the [Woodrow Wilson Teaching Fellowship](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson_Teaching_Fellowship "Woodrow Wilson Teaching Fellowship") and the [Presidential Management Fellowship](/wiki/Presidential_Management_Fellowship "Presidential Management Fellowship"). It is granted to prospective or current students, on the basis of their academic or research achievements.

In the UK, research fellowships are awarded to support [postdoctoral researchers](/wiki/Postdoctoral_research "Postdoctoral research") such as those funded by the [Wellcome Trust](/wiki/Wellcome_Trust "Wellcome Trust")[[10]](#cite_note-10) and the [Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Research Council (BBSRC)](/wiki/Biotechnology_and_Biological_Sciences_Research_Council "Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Research Council").[[11]](#cite_note-11) At [ETH Zurich](/wiki/ETH_Zurich "ETH Zurich"), postdoctoral fellowships support incoming researchers.[[12]](#cite_note-eth-12) The [MacArthur Fellows Program](/wiki/MacArthur_Fellows_Program "MacArthur Fellows Program") (aka "genius grant") as prestigious research fellowship awarded in the United States.

### Fellowships as a training program

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Fellowships as a training program")]

Fellowships may involve a short placement for capacity building,[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) e.g., to get more experience in government, such as the [American Association for the Advancement of Science](/wiki/American_Association_for_the_Advancement_of_Science "American Association for the Advancement of Science")'s fellowships[[15]](#cite_note-15) and the [American Academy of Arts and Sciences Fellowship programs](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences#Fellowship_programs "American Academy of Arts and Sciences"). Some institutions offer fellowships as a professional training program as well as a financial grant, such as the [Balsillie School of International Affairs](/wiki/Balsillie_School_of_International_Affairs "Balsillie School of International Affairs"), where tuition and other fees are paid by the fellowship.

### Fellowships as a special membership grade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Fellowships as a special membership grade")]

Fellows are often the highest grade of membership of many [professional associations](/wiki/Professional_association "Professional association") or [learned societies](/wiki/Learned_society "Learned society"), for example, the [Chartered Institute of Arbitrators](/wiki/Chartered_Institute_of_Arbitrators "Chartered Institute of Arbitrators"), the [Chartered Governance Institute](/wiki/Chartered_Governance_Institute "Chartered Governance Institute"), [Royal College of Surgeons](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Surgeons "Royal College of Surgeons"), the [Institution of Chemical Engineers](/wiki/Institution_of_Chemical_Engineers "Institution of Chemical Engineers"), or [Royal Society of Chemistry](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_Chemistry "Royal Society of Chemistry"). Lower grades are referred to as members (who typically share voting rights with the fellows), or associates (who may or may not, depending on whether "associate" status is a form of full membership).
Additional grades of membership exist in, for example, the [Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers](/wiki/Institute_of_Electrical_and_Electronics_Engineers#Membership_grades "Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers") (IEEE) and the [Association for Computing Machinery](/wiki/Association_for_Computing_Machinery#Membership_grades "Association for Computing Machinery") (ACM).

Fellowships of this type can be awarded as a [title of honor](/wiki/Title_of_honor "Title of honor") in their own right, e.g. the [Fellow of the Royal Society](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society") (FRS) or [Fellow of the Royal Academy of Engineering](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Academy_of_Engineering "Fellow of the Royal Academy of Engineering") (FREng).
Exclusive [learned societies](/wiki/Learned_society "Learned society") such as the [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society") have Fellow as the only grade of membership.

Appointment as an honorary fellow in a learned or professional society can be either to honour exceptional achievement or service within the professional domain of the awarding body or to honour contributions related to the domain from someone who is professionally outside it. Membership of the awarding body may or may not be a requirement.

How a fellowship is awarded varies for each society, but may typically involve some or all of these:

* A qualifying period in a lower grade
* Passing a series of examinations
* Nomination by two existing fellows who know the applicant professionally
* Evidence of continued formal training post-qualification
* Evidence of substantial achievement in the subject area
* Submission of a thesis or portfolio of works which will be examined
* Election by a vote of the fellowship

### In ancient universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In ancient universities")]

At the [ancient universities](/wiki/Ancient_universities "Ancient universities") of [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford"), [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"), and [Trinity College, Dublin](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Dublin "Trinity College, Dublin"), members of the teaching staff typically have two affiliations: one as a reader, lecturer, or other [academic rank](/wiki/Academic_rank "Academic rank") within a department of the university, as at other universities, and a second affiliation as a fellow of one of the [colleges](/wiki/Residential_college "Residential college") of the university.
The fellows, sometimes referred to as [university dons](/wiki/University_don "University don"), form the [governing body](/wiki/Governance "Governance") of the college. They may elect a council to handle day-to-day management. All fellows are entitled to certain privileges within their colleges, which may include dining at [High Table](/wiki/High_Table "High Table") (free of charge) and possibly the right to a room in college (free of charge).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At Cambridge, retired academics may remain fellows.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] At Oxford, however, a governing body fellow would normally be elected a *fellow emeritus* and would leave the governing body upon their retirement.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Distinguished old members of the college, or its benefactors and friends, might also be elected 'Honorary Fellow', normally for life; but beyond limited dining rights this is merely an honour. Most Oxford colleges have 'Fellows by Special Election' or 'Supernumerary Fellows', who may be members of the teaching staff, but not necessarily members of the governing body.

Some senior administrators of a college such as [bursars](/wiki/Bursar "Bursar") are made fellows, and thereby members of the governing body, because of their importance to the running of a college.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### In the U.S.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: In the U.S.")]

At some universities in the United States, "fellows" are members of the [board of trustees](/wiki/Board_of_governors "Board of governors") who hold administrative positions as non-executive [trustee](/wiki/Trustee "Trustee") rather than academics.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Industry and corporate fellows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Industry and corporate fellows")]

See also: [Category:Corporate fellows](/wiki/Category:Corporate_fellows "Category:Corporate fellows"), [IBM Fellow](/wiki/IBM_Fellow "IBM Fellow"), and [ICL Fellows](/wiki/ICL_Fellows "ICL Fellows")

In industries intensive in [science](/wiki/Science "Science"), [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering"), [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine"), and [research & development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development"), companies may appoint a very small number of top senior researchers as corporate, technical or industry fellows, either in [Science](/wiki/Science "Science") or in [Engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering"). These are internationally recognized leaders who are among the best in the world in their respective fields.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Corporate, Technical or Industry Fellow in either [Science](/wiki/Science "Science") or [Engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") is the most senior rank or title one can achieve in a scientific or engineering [career](/wiki/Career "Career"), though fellows often also hold business titles such as [Vice President](/wiki/Vice_President "Vice President")[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) or [Chief Technology Officer](/wiki/Chief_Technology_Officer "Chief Technology Officer").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Notable examples of fellows in scientific, medical and other research-intensive organizations include:

* [Advanced Micro Devices](/wiki/Advanced_Micro_Devices "Advanced Micro Devices") (AMD) Fellows[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* [Apple Fellows](/wiki/Apple_Inc.#Corporate_culture "Apple Inc.")[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* Battelle Fellows[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* [Bell Labs](/wiki/Bell_Labs "Bell Labs") Fellows[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* [Boeing Fellows](/wiki/Boeing_Technical_Fellowship "Boeing Technical Fellowship")
* [Deloitte Fellows](https://www2.deloitte.com/us/en/pages/careers/articles/careers-blog-introducing-technology-fellows.html)[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* [DXC Technology](/wiki/DXC_Technology "DXC Technology") Fellows[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* DuPont Fellows[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") fellows[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* [Henry Ford Technical Fellows](/wiki/Henry_Ford_Technical_Fellow "Henry Ford Technical Fellow")
* [HP](/wiki/HP_Inc "HP Inc") Fellows[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* Intel Fellows[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* [Microsoft Fellows](/wiki/Category:Microsoft_technical_fellows "Category:Microsoft technical fellows")
* [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") Fellows[[34]](#cite_note-34)
* [Oak Ridge National Laboratory](/wiki/Oak_Ridge_National_Laboratory "Oak Ridge National Laboratory") (ORNL) Fellows[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* RTI International fellows[[36]](#cite_note-36)
* Toray Fellows[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Nonprofit and government fellowships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Fellow&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Nonprofit and government fellowships")]

The title fellow can be used for participants in a professional development program run by a [nonprofit](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization") or governmental organization. This type of fellowship is a short term work opportunity (1–2 years)[[38]](#cite_note-Find_Non-Profit_Fellowships-38) for [professionals](/wiki/Professional "Professional") who already possess some level of academic or professional expertise that will serve the nonprofit mission. Fellows are given a [stipend](/wiki/Stipend "Stipend") as well as professional experience and leadership training.
